title: Isotope Geochemistry
objectives: This inter-disciplinary course aims to present application of (inorganic) chemistry in Earth and Climate sciences. We will discuss in detail how distribution of stable, radioactive and radiogenic isotopes can be used to understand various geological, oceanic and climatic processes. The goal is to understand the behaviour of selected isotopes in different geochemical reservoirs and applying these properties in the field of hydrology, ocean and climate sciences. The course will provide fundamental knowledge and applications related to radioactive, and stable isotopes, and will also develop this highly sought-after skillset for natural sciences.
course_content: Basics of isotopes, notations, isotopic standards* Nucleosynthesis* Mass spectrometer and Isotope dilution* Radioactivity decay* Secular equilibrium* Short-lived U-Th nuclides* Radiogenic (K-Ar, Ar-Ar, Rb-Sr, Sm-Nd, Re-Os, U- Pb) isotopic systematics* Binary mixing model* Stable (C,N,S,O) isotopic systematics* Application of these isotopes for dating, Paleo-thermometry* Hydrology* Provenance tracing* Atmospheric oxygenation* Submarine groundwater. Additionally, four three-hours experiments will be conducted to provide hands-on experience with ICPMS, Isotope dilution measurement and data handling.
evaluation: / Assessment a. End-sem examination- 35% b. Mid-sem examination- 35% c. Continuous Assessment – 30%
readings: 1. Faure, G. and Mensing, T.M. (2004) Isotopes: Principles and Applications, 3rd Edition, John Wiley publishers, 928 pp. 2. Dickin, A.P. (2005) Radiogenic Isotope Geology, 2nd Edition, Cambridge Publishers, 512 pp. 3. Allegre C. (2008), Isotope Geology, Cambridge University Press, 512 pp. 4. Sharp Z. (2007) Principles of stable isotope geochemistry, 2nd edition, Pearson/Prentice Hal, 344 pp.

title: Thermodynamics
objectives: Thermodynamics deals with the changes in matter and inter- conversion of various forms of energy. Classical thermodynamics is based on phenomenological observations and statistical thermodynamics provides a molecular basis. The objective of this course to introduce both aspects of thermodynamics necessary to understand various process of relevance to physics, chemistry and biology. The various laws of thermodynamics, the mathematical relations and applications will be discussed in this course. The outcome from this course will serve as foundation for understanding reactions and mechanisms.
course_content: Thermodynamics in everyday life, System and surroundings, macroscopic and microscopic systems, concept of equilibrium, thermodynamic state of the system. Zeroth law, gas thermometers, equation of state, real gas and virial equation (4 lectures) First law of thermodynamics, work, heat, internal energy, equivalence of heat and work, expansion/ compression work, isothermal processes, reversible processes, Joule’s free expansion, adiabatic changes, specific heats, enthalpy, Joule-Thompson experiment (5 lectures) Thermochemistry, calorimetry (2 lectures) Second Law of thermodynamics, concept of entropy, equivalence of the statements of second law, heat engines and efficiency (internal combustion engine and external combustion engine), refrigerators, Carnot cycle (4 lectures) Definition of entropy, Clausius inequality, calculation of entropy (2 lectures) Statistical formulation of second law, Microscopic interpretation of entropy, concept of probability, microstates and distribution, two level and multi level systems, distinguishable and indistinguishable particles, most probable distribution, Boltzmann distribution, estimation of entropy of various processes, microscopic equivalent of heat and work, partition function (7 lectures) Fundamental equation, Legendre transformation, Introduction to free energy, Criteria for spontaneous change, Maxwell relations and applications, general thermodynamic relations using Jacobian method (5 lectures) Applications of free energy (1 lecture)
evaluation: / Assessment End-Sem Examination: 40% Mid-Sem Examination: 40% Others - 20 %
readings: Silbey, R., R. Alberty, and M. Bawendi. Physical Chemistry. 4th ed. New York, NY: John Wiley & Sons, 2004. Atkins, P., and J. de Paula. Physical Chemistry. 7th ed. New York, NY: W.H. Freeman and Company, 2001. Hanson, R.M., Green, S.. Introduction to Molecular Thermodynamics. University Science Books, 2008.

title: Organotransition Metal Catalysis
objectives: Catalysis is of fundamental importance in chemistry and plays a vital role in basic research, applied sciences as well as industrial processes. To understand the essence of catalysis and relationship between catalysis effect and catalyst structure, it is important to learn the basic principles behind the metal, ligands and their bonding properties. Emphasis will be placed on structure, bonding and catalytic cycles and how they are derived from appropriate experimental data* implicit in this is a complete understanding of the fundamental reaction types prevalent in these cycles such as oxidative addition, migratory insertion and reductive elimination* How appropriate ligand design can be used to ‘tailor’ catalytic properties such as reactivity and selectivity. It will serve as a foundation to apply this knowledge for better understanding of catalysts and catalysis.
course_content: 1. Introduction: Catalysis - Term and basic principles, Why is catalysis required?, The development of organotransition metal catalysis, Principles of catalysis: homogenous and heterogeneous catalysis, selectivity and specificity of catalysts, general practical method for catalysis, etc. (4 hrs) 2. Structure and bonding: General properties of ligands: dative (L-type), covalent (X-type), neutral, anionic, even- or odd electron, pi-bonded, combination of sigma and pi donor, and cationic ligands. Structure and electronic properties of phosphines and N-heterocyclic carbenes, estimation of their donor strength, Tolman electronic parameter, ligands effect on catalytic efficiency. Properties of metals: oxidation state, the relationship between oxidation state and no. of d-electrons, trends in the properties of transition metals. (8 hrs) 3. Homogeneous Catalysis and Important reactions: Elementary steps in organometallic catalysis: oxidative addition and reductive elimination, agostic interaction, beta- hydrogen elimination, and sigma-bond metathesis. Overview of reactions catalyzed by metal complexes in a solution. A detailed discussion of the major homogeneous catalytic reactions, including hydrogenation, carbonylation, hydroformylation, hydrocyanation, hydrosilylation, hydroamination, and hydroboration reactions, and a brief overview of coupling reactions, including C-C and C-N coupling reactions. (10 hrs) 4. Asymmetric Catalysis: Introduction, general principle, and practical aspects of asymmetric catalysis. (3 hrs) 5. Heterogeneous Catalysis: General features, basic methods of synthesis and characterization, Ziegler-Natta catalysis: oligomerization and polymerization, Ammonia Synthesis, Fischer-Tropsch synthesis, Methane reforming and the mechanism, Zeolite catalysis, Hydrodesulfurization, Selective oxidation of propylene, Kinetics in heterogeneous catalytic reactions (8 hrs). 6. Non-classical Catalysis: Introduction of non-classical catalysis, Catalysts based on main group elements, Frustrated Lewis pair catalysts (FLPs): Basic fundamentals of FLPs and their application in small molecules activation and reduction of many functional groups, Organocatalysis: types of catalysts, examples, and mechanism, Lanthanides in catalysis, Alkali and alkaline earth metal-catalyzed reactions and mechanism. (8 hrs)
evaluation: / Assessment a. End-sem examination- 35% b. Mid-sem examination-35% c. Quiz- 30%
readings: 1. Dirk Steinborn, Fundamentals of Organometallic Catalysis, Wiley-VCH . 2. John F. Hartwig, Organotransition Metal Chemistry. From Bonding to Catalysis, University Science Books, Mill Valley, California. 3. Matthias Beller, A. Renken, R. A. van Santen Catalysis. From Principles to Applications, Wiley-VCH. 4. Gadi Rothenberg, Catalysis. Concepts and Green Applications. Wiley-VCH. 5. For Asymmetric Catalysis: Walsh, P. J., Kozlowski, M. C., Fundamentals of Asymmetric Catalysis, University Science Books, California, 2008.

title: Research Methods in Science Education
objectives: This course is mainly designed for students who are considering MS projects in science education. This course will introduce a variety of research methods before they conduct a research project in Science Education. Additionally, this course will be useful for students who are interested in understanding the advances in and research frontiers of the field of science education. Moreover, this course will give a good exposure to conduct theoretically grounded and empirically rigorous educational research. Students will study case studies of research in science education in India and across the globe. They will also work in groups to propose a research project, write it as a research grant proposal and present it. Learning outcomes: By the end of this course students will be able to 1. Read an article in science education and evaluate it critically 2. Have a broad understanding of research methodologies deployed in science education research 3. Study and present at least one kind of research methodology in depth 4. Prepare a research proposal in science education
course_content: Unit 1: Introduction (4 lectures) ? Prior ideas ? History of science education research ? Introduction to main domains: School students (studying students thinking and learning process, designing intervention) higher education (DBER), teacher education ? Reviewing and summarizing literature Unit 2: Research Questions and Research Methodologies (3 lectures) ? Overview of main research questions in the area of science education ? Identifying a good research topic ? Asking right kind of research questions ? Identifying suitable methodologies ? Sampling methods ? Tool and procedures for collecting qualitative and quantitative data Unit 3: Research Ethics (2 lectures) Unit 4: Research Designs (13 lectures) Case studies ? Descriptive / historical / ethnographic ? Curriculum analysis ? Secondary data analysis (Meta analysis / large data sets - correlational / factor analysis) ? Exploratory research ? Action Research ? Experimental / quasi-experimental Research ? DBR (Research questions, claims, methods, data analysis and contributions of each of this research design will be discussed with an example) Unit 5: Presenting Your Research (3 lectures) ? How to prepare a poster / write an abstract / a report ? Some important societies and journals ? Some prominent researchers or labs in the world and the research done by them
evaluation: / Assessment Group presentations (10%): A group of students will be given a paper or a set of papers and will be asked to study and present the research methodology Online discussion forum (10%): Responses to online prompts Assignment / quiz (15%) Research Proposals (30%): Right in the beginning students will be told that they have to write a proposal by the end of this course. During each unit we will help students to write those parts. The draft proposal which is to be submitted around mid-sem will carry 10% weightage and the final proposal will carry 20% weightage. End-sem exam - 35%

objectives: Prepare students for further studies in (theoretical and observational) high energy astrophysics, plasma astrophysics, stellar physics and supernovae, etc.

title: Calculus II
objectives: This course is aimed at providing a concise introduction to the calculus of the vector-valued functions of several variables. It is useful for the students who want to study topics like Differential Geometry, Topology, Analysis, Lie groups, Differential Equations, Theory of Relativity, Quantum Mechanics, Mathematical Biology, etc. The goal is to get the students acquainted with the basic notions of partial and directional derivatives, multiple integrals, line and surface integrals of functions of several variables.
course_content: Euclidean space, Pythagoras* Theorem, Distance between two points, Functions on Rn, Limit and continuity of functions on Rn, Sufficient conditions for continuity, Partial and directional derivatives, Total derivative, Sufficient conditions for differentiability, Higher order partial derivatives, Sufficient condition for change of order of differentiation, Equation of tangent plane to a differentiable surface, Chain rule, Jacobian Matrix, Taylor*s Theorem, Implicit function theorem, Inverse function theorem, Local optima, Lagrange multiplier, Curves, Line integrals, Regular domain, Multiple integrals, Fubini*s theorem, Differentiation under the integral sign, Green*s theorem, Path independent integral.
evaluation: / Assessment End-sem examination 35% Mid-sem examination 35% Quiz 30%

title: Transition metal Chemistry
objectives: The objective of this course is to provide a detailed account to the chemistry of transition metals and emphasize their relationship to other multi-disciplinary topics such as bioinorganic chemistry and organometallic chemistry. The central theme of this course is to focus on the fundamental concepts needed to understand transition metal chemistry, relevant to their structure, bonding, properties such as spectral characterizes, reactivity, stereochemistry etc. This course will be useful for all those students, who have opted for chemistry as a major subject. At the end of this course, students will also learn about the role of transition metals in several other fields like materials science, biology and catalysis.
course_content: 1. Introduction to the Transition Metal Chemistry ((1 Lecture) 2. Nature of Metal-Ligand Bonding: Crystal and ligand field theories. Crystal field stabilization energies, Irving Williams series, 10Dq and pairing energies, Jahn-Teller Distortion, High spin and Low spin complexes. Magnetism, Molecular orbital (MO) theory of Octahedral, Tetrahedral and Square Planar complexes. Nephelauxetic Series. Pi bonding and MO Theory (8-10 lectures). 3. Inorganic Spectroscopy: Spectroscopic terms, LS coupling. Selection rules, Orgel diagrams, Tanabe-Sugano diagrams. Charge transfer transitions (10-12 lectures) 4. Lanthanides and Actinides: Coordination chemistry, Spectra and magnetic properties. (1-2 lectures) 5. Stability and Reactions of Coordination Compounds: Thermodynamic and kinetic stability, Labile and inert complexes, Ligand substitutions in octahedral and square planar complexes, Trans-Effect. Mechanism of redox reactions, Inner and outer sphere mechanisms, Photochemical reactions. (8-10 lectures) 6. Basics of Metal-organic Frameworks (MOFs) and Related Porous Materials. (1-2 lectures) 7. Basics of Organometallic Chemistry and Catalysis: The 18-electron rule, Bonding, Hapticity, Oxidation numbers and stability. Carbonyls, Nitrosyls, Alkyls, Alkylidines, Allyl and cyclic polyene complexes, Metal-metal bonds, Metallocenes, Fluxionality, Organometallic reactions, Catalysis. (6-9 lectures) 8. Basics of Bioinorganic Chemistry: Roles of metals in biology, introduction to photosynthesis, Oxygen transport and storage by heme and nonheme proteins, Physical methods to study bioinorganic systems. (1-2 lectures)
evaluation: / Assessment a. End-Sem Examination-50% b. Mid-Sem Examination-30% c. Quiz (two): 20% Will be communicated in the class if there is any change.
readings: Text Book(s) 1. Inorganic Chemistry by Huheey, Keiter, Keiter, Medhi (4th Ed.) 2. Inorganic Chemistry by Shriver & Atkins (4th Ed.) 3. Concise Inorganic Chemistry by J.D. Lee (5th Ed.)

title: Computational functional genomics
prerequisites: Bioinformatics course + Proficiency in programming + Good knowledge of probability and statistics*
objectives: The primary objective is to impart a comprehensive understanding of bio-technologies and methods that help characterize functional regions of the genome. Focus will be on (a) technologies that measure genome output and (b) algorithmic understanding of methods that infer how genome output is regulated. We will look at various types of high-throughput technologies that measure genome output, both in bulk and in single-cells. Students are expected to develop a deep understanding of the statistical and computational aspects of modern methods that learn Biology from these data.
course_content: High-throughput assays developed in the past two decades that measure genome output: active transcription, active translation, TF-DNA binding, protein-RNA binding, local chromatin structure, chromatin looping. All these assays will be studied in both bulk and single cell context. In the computational side: enumerative, statistical, and deep learning methods for analysing data from these experiments will be discussed. There will be a project component, for which proficiency in programming is expected.
evaluation: / Assessment End Sem = 30% Mid Sem = 30% Others (assignments + project) = 40%
readings: Course will be designed around the latest literature in the field. Recent publications will form the bulk of the course material.

Riemann mapping theorem

title: Disasters and Society
prerequisites: None except for discipline for reading and writing.
objectives: This course provides an introduction to the social dimensions of disaster events. It will examine how disasters (natural, technological, and human-made) are social events. It will cover key concepts and perspectives on risk, resilience, and vulnerability in the context of core developmental issues. It will enhance student understanding of how culture, social structure, and inequality influence people’s preparedness, capacities, and responses to disasters. This also builds an appreciation of the dynamic between social change and disaster. Learning Outcomes: By the end of the course students will be able to: 1. Learn basic terms, concepts, and social theories for understanding disaster events 2. Apply these terms and perspectives to understand disasters 3. Think critically about how/why disasters are social events that both shape and are shaped by social change.
course_content: I. Introduction: Definitions and Debates (Disaster, Development, Their nexus) II. Concepts and II. Critical perspectives: Hazards, Risk, Vulnerability, Resilience, Response, and Recovery III. Social Science theories of disaster: Social systems Theory, Social vulnerability Theory, Social constructionism Theory, Hazard perception & risk communication Theory, Complexity and Normal Accident Theory, Risk Society, Disaster Capitalism IV. Intersectionality and its discontents (Race, Caste, Class, Gender, Caste, Age, Ability, etc) V. Popular culture and disaster (Media, Memory, and Behavioral response and myth) VI. Lessons from Disasters: Exploring Pathways for Justice
evaluation: / Assessment Mid Sem Assessment: 40% End Sem Assessment: 40% Continuous Assessment: 20%
readings: The course will utilize a combination of core and supplementary reading materials. All readings will be accessible online. Please refer to HS3114 Google Classroom for the weekly topic, readings, and other details.

title: Evolutionary Biology
objectives: This course aims to provide an understanding of the central principles, mechanisms, and evidences underlying evolutionary biology. It is designed to be relevant for those seeking a deeper appreciation of the fundamental framework unifying all biological knowledge. Expected Outcome Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to: *Analyze and articulate the compelling lines of evidence supporting evolution and critically discuss the historical development of evolutionary concepts. *Apply quantitative models (e.g., HWE, selection models) to predict changes in allele and genotype frequencies in populations under the influence of various evolutionary forces. *Describe the evolutionary causes and consequences of biological phenomena such as sexual reproduction, altruism, speciation, and species-level diversity. *Integrate knowledge across multiple scales—from molecular and genomic evolution to micro- and macroevolution. *Utilize an evolutionary framework to interpret biological observations, address contemporary issues and contribute to a deeper understanding of the living world.
course_content: - Overview of evolution, History of evolutionary thought - Evidence for evolution - Variation. Mutations. Plasticity, canalization and genetic assimilation, Non-genetic inheritances. HWE for one and two locus systems. - Selection, 1-locus, 2-allele viability selection model. - Genetic Drift, migration, mating systems, effective population size. - Why sex? Sexual selection and its various types, Sexual conflicts. - Eusociality, Altruism, Kin selection, Parent-offspring conflicts, Siblicide. - Species concepts, Speciation, Reproductive isolation, adaptive radiation. - Birth and death of genes, - Origin of life and human evolution - Evolutionary medicine, Use of evolutionary thought beyond biology.
evaluation: / Assessment End Sem = 40%, Mid Sem = 40%, Assignment = 20%
readings: No one textbook can be prescribed. Material will be provided from multiple sources.

title: Riemannian Geometry
objectives: Riemannian Geometry is the study of smooth manifolds equipped with Riemannian metric. This metric can be used to define the geometry of lengths and area on submanifolds of M, and moreover notions of geodesics, curvature and parallel transport. This course will introduce you to the delicate interplay between curvature and topology. This is an important course for both future topologists and theoretical physicists.
course_content: Vector bundles, Tensors, Riemannian metrics, Model spaces (space forms), Shape operator, Affine Connections, Riemannian connections, Gaussian curvature, Gauss Theorema Egregium, Connections on vector bundles, Curvature tensor, Parallel transport, Geodesics, Exponential maps, Hopf-Rinow theorem, Geodesic curvature, Gauss-Bonnet theorem.
evaluation: / Assessment End Sem 35%, Mid Sem 35%, Continuous evaluation (Assignments, Quizzes etc.) 30%
readings: 1. Differential Geometry: Connections, Curvature and Characteristic Classes. Loring W. Tu (2017), Springer GTM 2. Riemannian Manifolds: An Introduction to Curvature, John Lee (1997), Springer GTM 3. Introduction to Differentiable Manifolds and Riemannian Geometry: W. M.Boothby (2003) Academic Press

title: Fourier Analysis
objectives: Classical Fourier Analysis studies the phenomenon that many periodic functions on real line can be expanded as a Fourier series in terms of sine and cosines and as a continuous analogue there is a notion of Fourier transform. In this course, you will learn the mathematical theory of Fourier analysis in terms of L^2 spaces and Schwartz spaces.
course_content: Basic theory of Fourier series on circle: Fourier series of periodic functions on real line, orthogonality of Sines and Cosines, criterion for convergence of Fourier series, example of expansions in Sine and Cosine series, Fourier se- ries w.r.t. orthogonal system, convergence in the mean, Fourier series expansion of absolutely integrable functions: sufficient condition for convergence at continuity points. Fourier transform on R^n: Plancherel theorem, Schwartz space, Fourier inversion, Poisson summation formula, Paley-Wiener theorems for Fourier transform Topological vector spaces, Distributions, Tempered distributions, application of Fourier transform: solving heat and wave equation.
evaluation: / Assessment End Sem: 35%, Mid Sem: 35%, Others: 30%
readings: 1. Fourier Analysis An Introduction: Stein & Shakarachi, Princeton University Press 2. Fundamentals of Fourier analysis by Loukas Grafakos 3. Fourier analysis and its applications: G. B. Folland, AMS. 4. Topics in Functional Analysis and Applications: Kesavan, New Age International, Limited.

title: Statistical learning and data science
objectives: Data science has recently received a lot of attention in all of science, in part due to spectacular advances not only in the availability of large amounts of data but also in our ability to store and process it. There is a growing optimism across various science communities that data science methods can complement ab initio modelling in the discovery of fundamental scientific laws. Data science is a formalism of evidence-structured discovery, which employs a compendium of several tools from statistics, machine learning, optimization theory, and others, and can be used effectively on both ‘large’ and ‘small’ problems. This course will introduce students to data science to: (1) Empower them with a sharp knowledge of the domain and scope of data science, and (2) Train them in its methods so that they can discover how to make the most use of these tools in their own fields. Outcomes This course aims to introduce the essentials of data science in a broad manner that highlights: • Central principles and commonly used techniques. • Its wide applicability across multiple science disciplines, with special focus on biology and medicine.
course_content: • Introduction to statistical learning, problem formulation, and assessing model accuracy. PAC learning. The regression and classification settings, supervised and unsupervised learning, bias-variance tradeoff. • Methods: Linear and polynomial regression, K-nearest neighbors, classification, resampling methods, model selection and regularization, tree-based methods, boosting, kernel methods, and support vector machines. • If time permits, neural networks and unsupervised learning will be introduced, but not covered in detail. • Note: Reinforcement learning is NOT part of this course.
evaluation: / Assessment a. End-semester examination – 35% b. Mid-semester examination – 35% c. Continuous Assessment and Quizzes – 30%
readings: **Main Text:** An Introduction to Statistical Learning: with Applications in R (Springer Texts in Statistics) by James, Witten, Hastie, and Tibshirani. Springer, 2nd Edition, 2021. https://www.statlearning.com/ **Additional Texts:** 1. The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction, Second Edition. Authors: Hastie, Trevor* Tibshirani, Robert* Friedman, Jerome. Springer Series in Statistics, 2009. 2. Learning From Data. Authors: Yaser S. Abu-Mostafa, Malik Magdon-Ismail, Hsuan-Tien. title: Geophysical Fluid Dynamics
objectives: The course introduces the fluid dynamical laws governing the different scales of motion in the atmosphere and Ocean. It deals with the governing equations and approximations used under different conditions. Geophysical Fluid Dynamics is the foundation for understanding the movement of air and oceanic currents. The course provides a quantitative framework for explaining different classes of geophysical disturbances, their evolution, interaction with other disturbances and how they eventually decay.

title: Statistical Learning and Data Science
objectives: Data science has recently received a lot of attention in all of science, in part due to spectacular advances not only in the availability of large amounts of data but also in our ability to store and process it. There is a growing optimism across various science communities that data science methods can complement ab initio modelling in the discovery of fundamental scientific laws. Data science is a formalism of evidence-structured discovery, which employs a compendium of several tools from statistics, machine learning, optimization theory, and others, and can be used effectively on both ‘large’ and ‘small’ problems. This course will introduce students to data science to: (1) Empower them with a sharp knowledge of the domain and scope of data science, and (2) Train them in its methods so that they can discover how to make the most use of these tools in their own fields. Outcomes This course aims to introduce the essentials of data science in a broad manner that highlights: • Central principles and commonly used techniques. • Its wide applicability across multiple science disciplines, with special focus on biology and medicine.
course_content: • Introduction to statistical learning, problem formulation, and assessing model accuracy. PAC learning. The regression and classification settings, supervised and unsupervised learning, bias-variance tradeoff. • Methods: Linear and polynomial regression, K-nearest neighbors, classification, resampling methods, model selection and regularization, tree-based methods, boosting, kernel methods, and support vector machines. • If time permits, neural networks and unsupervised learning will be introduced, but not covered in detail. • Note: Reinforcement learning is NOT part of this course.
evaluation: / Assessment a. End-semester examination – 35% b. Mid-semester examination – 35% c. Continuous Assessment and Quizzes – 30%

title: Animal Behaviour
objectives: Animals sense the environment and their own internal state and respond with a variety of different behaviors that are appropriate for the situation. How and why do animals produce their different behaviors? As part of this course, students will be introduced to the scientific study of animal behavior with diverse examples from the animal kingdom. Half the course will deal with understanding the evolutionary approach to understanding *why* animals behave the way they do. The other half of the course will deal with how neurons, neural circuits, genes control *how* animals behave the way they do. Both of these are inter-related and both are essential for a better understanding of the subject. Material from both sections of the course will be interleaved during the course with a few weeks of studying the *why* and a few weeks of studying the *how* of animal behavior. Students will also be introduced to the methods used to study both aspects. Having taken the course, students can expect to learn the following: • Learn the basic steps of the scientific method - the process of making hypothesis, predictions and designing experiments to answer questions. • Understand the methods used to study questions about *how* and *why* animals behave the way they do • Understand and be able to apply the evolutionary approach to addressing *why* animals perform behaviors • Understand and be able to describe how neural circuits/genes interact to produce specific natural behaviors (for a few different example behaviors that will be discussed) Anyone curious about animal behavior will find this course useful. In addition, the study of animal behavior is useful to a number of other fields including conservation biology, evolution, ecology, neuroscience, etc...
course_content: 1. Introduction to the scientific method and addressing questions in animal behavior (1-2 lectures) 2. Tinbergen’s four questions (1 lecture) 3. Phylogenetic analysis and game theory (2-3 lectures) 4. Sampling methods - 1 lectures 5. Intro to neurophysiology - 2 lectures 6. Proximate questions in animal behaviour with discussion of the specific models - 15 lectures. Some models that will be discussed are given below. This is subject to some change. a) prey discrimination by the toad b) sound localization by barn owls c) sound production by crickets d) song production in songbirds 7. Ultimate mechanisms of behaviour and the evolutionary approach to studying behaviour (~15 lectures). Some topics that will be discussed are given below. Some topics are subject to change. a) Behavioral ecology and sociobiology – group selection theory, kin selection, altruistic behavior b) Social and cultural transmission of Behavior c) Evolution of anti-predator behaviour, feeding behaviours and communication d) Evolutionary ecology of reproductive behavior, mating systems and parental care in animals
evaluation: / Assessment Midsem exam - 30% Endsem exam - 30% Others - 40% Others includes semester-long project, quizzes and other assignments.

title: Classical mechanics (E )
objectives: To make you learn the fundamentals of Classical Mechanics.
course_content: So far, you have studied Newton’s laws and equations of motion, where you identify the forces, write down the equations of motion and solve them to understand the dynamics of a system. However, this process becomes complicated for systems with numerous constraints, and one naturally asks if there exists a general way of constructing such equations and under what conditions they are generally solved. In this course, we will discuss the general formalisms using Lagrangian and Hamiltonian. Using these methods, we will study two classes of motion: (a) small oscillations of a system about equilibrium positions and (b) motion of two bodies moving under the influence of mutual interactions. These are two general classes of motion which are exactly solvable. This is a foundational course for physics students, but it could also appeal to those majoring in mathematics. This course will involve mathematical details beyond what you are familiar with so far in the context of classical mechanics.
evaluation: / Assessment Mid-semester: 35%, End-semester: 35%, and two quizzes before and after mid-semester, each worth 15%.

Students should also be able to use these ideas to calculate the statistical properties of simple systems.

Review of probability: one random variable, probability distributions, random walks, many random variables, central limit theorem, information, entropy and estimation. title: Carbon Nanotube, Graphene and Quantum Hall Effect
objectives: This course aims to cover both theoretical and experimental aspects pertaining to electron transport in 1D and 2D systems, with carbon nanotube for 1D and graphene for 2D as model systems. This would enable students to explore the wonders of reduced dimensionality, need of low temperature & high magnetic field for exploring quantized transport. This would also convey how a low temperature experiment in a 2D electron gas eventually led to redefining universal standard for resistance units and inspired a paradigm shift in condensed matter physics.
course_content: This course would cover band structure calculations using tight binding model in graphene and how this approach is helpful towards understanding band structure of rolled up sheet of graphene - that is CNT. Implications of such calculations in electrical transport by highlighting experimental data of Von hove Singularities in CNT. Quantum Hall Effect and Landau levels. Experimental observation of QHE in semiconductor based heterostructures along with the technical challenges in preparation of 2D electron gas and finally experimental observation of QHE in graphene. Course would be divided in 3 parts, CNT, Graphene and QHE, covering essential basics for each topic. 12 lectures.

title: Stochastic Processes
prerequisites: Real Analysis-I and II, Measure Theory and Integration, Probability (BSMS semester 7)
objectives: One comes across many random quantities in their life e.g. waiting time at customer service, daily rainfall, stock prices, emission of particles. In the previous probability courses, you have learnt a mathematical way to study such quantities using sample spaces, random variables and their distributions. In this course, you will systematically study the dynamical aspects e.g. sample paths, long term behaviour of families of random variables indexed by a time parameter (discrete / continuous). The main objectives of this course includes studying discrete /continuous time Markov processes (e.g. Markov chains, Poisson process), Martingales (e.g. random walk) and continuous time/space processes (e.g. Wiener process a.k.a. the Brownian motion). These examples and the general theory that drives them is the core of “Stochastic processes and Stochastic analysis”. You will learn the basic foundational results like Kolmogorov consistency theorem and weak convergence along the way. You will also learn the basic tools of Ito Calculus and Stochastic differential equations in the last part of the course. This course will particularly benefit anyone interested to study queuing theory, mathematical finance, stochastic game theory, and other fields of stochastic analysis.
course_content: Basic theory of stochastic processes, Filtration of sigma- algebras, Stopping time, Finite dimensional distributions of a stochastic process, Space of sample paths, Measure on the path space, CADLAG (a.k.a. RCLL) processes (~4 lectures) Theory of Markov chains (discrete/continuous time) Chapman-Kolmogorov equations, Classification of states, Mean recurrence times for discrete time Markov chains, Stationary distributions, Limit theorems, Continuous time Markov chains, Embedded discrete time chain, holding times, Infinitesimal generator of a continuous time Markov chain (rate matrix), backward and forward equations, Poisson process: Axiomatic definition, derivation of distribution, Waiting times and their distribution, Properties of finite dimensional distributions, Strong Markov property and other properties of Poisson process (~6 lectures) Existence and Properties of Wiener process (Brownian motion), Markov property, stopping times and the strong Markov property of Wiener process, Reflection principle, First passage time, Wiener measure on C[0,1], properties of sample paths (~10 Lectures) Theory of Martingales, Doob’s inequality, Stopping times and optional sampling theorem, Convergence theorems for submartingales, Doob-Meyer decomposition and examples (~6-8 Lectures) Introduction to Stochastic calculus and stochastic differential equations, Ito integral of Simple processes and approximation, Definition and properties of Ito integral, Ito’s isometry theorem, Ito’s formula (statement and applications) (~8-10 Lectures).

title: From Planets to cells
prerequisites: Basic background in biochemistry, earth sciences and organic chemistry will be useful but is not mandatory.
objectives: To give a comprehensive overview of how life came about on Earth, starting from how the stage was set to how chemistry could have transitioned to biology on the early Earth. The former will involve aspects of planetary sciences/geology and geochemistry, to give the students a background of how life as we know it was set to emerge on the Earth. The latter will involve gaining insights into the prebiotic chemistry that was feasible, given our understanding of the geochemical settings, and how that period transitioned to biological chemistry, setting the stage for the emergence and early evolution of life on our planet. This course is open to BS-MS 6th & 8th semesters, to iPhD and PhD students who are interested in learning about how life could have chemically originated on the early Earth. Outcomes: This course aims to give students an appreciation of how using a multi-disciplinary approach allows for a comprehensive understanding of how life would have originated on the early Earth. It will also introduce students to thinking from across science disciplines pertaining to the course content (detailed below).
course_content: To give a comprehensive overview of how life came about on Earth, starting from how the stage was set, to how chemistry transitioned to biology on the early Earth. The former will involve aspects of planetary sciences/geochemistry, to give the students an overview of how life as we know it was set to emerge on the Earth. The latter will involve giving insights into the prebiotic chemistry that was feasible, given our understanding of the geochemical settings. Subsequently, we will discuss how the transition from abiotic to minimal life could have panned out that set the stage for the emergence and early evolution of life on our planet. I. Planets and life (22 classes): - Planetary diversity: Different kinds of planets. - Planet formation: How, and when? - Introduction to Cosmochemistry - Properties of the Terrestrial planets - What makes a planet Habitable? How will we know? - Habitable planets: The role of the star and the planet - Definition of life and search for Life in the Universe II. Geochemical settings that set the stage for life’s emergence (~3 classes): - Early Earth bombardment history and implications for life’s emergence - Energy Sources available for reactions - Geological niches that would have supported the origins of life III. Chemical origins of life (~4 classes) - Major theories on transition from non-life to life - Oparin and Haldane’s hypothesis, Urey Miller Experiment - Terrestrial syntheses and exogeneous delivery of prebiotic chemicals - Current OoL Theories: Simplicity to complexity and top- down/bottom-up approaches IV. Prebiotic chemistry (~6 classes) - Introduction to prebiotic chemistry: Prebiotic inventory tied to theories – the historical perspective (soup, heterotrophy versus autotrophy) - Prebiotic chemistry leading to the four different classes of extant biomolecules. - Various “First” Theories: RNA World, Protein World, Lipid World, Metabolism World and Role of minerals* what were their shortcomings - Alternative systems (diff from what we see in extant biology) IV. Transition from prebiotic to biotic world using RNA World as the backdrop (~7 classes) - Nonenzymatic replication of RNA - RNA and ribozymes in the emergence/early evolution of life - Importance of compartmentalization (membrane-bound and membrane-less systems) - Discuss implications of compartmentalization for Darwinian evolution.
evaluation: / Assessment a. Up to Mid Sem - 50% b. Continuous assessment - 20% c. End Sem - 30% The course could involve rigorous and involved oral presentation evaluations.
readings: 1. First Life – Discovering the Connections Between Stars, Cells and How Life Began by David Deamer 2. The Emergence of Life – From Chemical Origins to Synthetic Biology by Pier Luigi Luisi 3. Lectures in Astrobiology Parts 1 and II by Bernard Barbier, Muriel Gargaud, Hervé Martin and Jacques Reisse 4. Young Sun, Early Earth and the Origins of Life: Lessons for Astrobiology by Muriel Gargaud, Hervé Martin, Purificación López-García, Thierry Montmerle, Robert Pascal and Storm Dunlop (Translator) 5. Peer-reviewed articles and reviews from relevant literature.

title: Genome Biology
prerequisites: Introductory Biology, Note: Students who have taken *Genome Biology and Epigenetics* are not eligible to take this course
objectives: This course aims to introduce students to the fundamental concepts of gene regulation, chromatin biology, genome- environment interactions, and epigenetics, along with their applications in genomics and disease biology. Since the release of the human genome sequence in 2003, there have been groundbreaking discoveries and technological advancements that have transformed our understanding of genome organization and function. Within the nucleus, the genome is packaged with basic proteins to form chromatin, with nucleosomes serving as its repeating structural units. The structure of chromatin plays a crucial role in maintaining heritable patterns of gene expression. The course will explore the mechanisms by which the genome and epigenome impact development, cellular differentiation, and complex diseases, using specific examples for illustration. Course Goals: • Introduce students to the latest advances and ongoing developments in genome biology, particularly through the integration of high-resolution imaging and genomics-based approaches. • Provide valuable insights for any student interested in the fundamental workings of our genomes and how recent interdisciplinary findings are being applied to understand both normal and diseased cells. These advancements are paving the way for improved diagnosis and treatment of complex human diseases, such as cancer. • Equip students with the ability to design novel strategies and assays that leverage the power of genomics to investigate both fundamental and applied questions in biology. Discussions will integrate perspectives from biochemistry, cell biology, computational biology, and systems biology.
course_content: Module:1: Cell biology of the nucleus – Nuclear envelope, Nuclear pore complexes (Nups), Nuclear bodies, nuclear speckles and their functional significance, Mechanobiology, Phase separation in the nucleus and how these processes shape the genome (7) Module:2: Advances in imaging the genome – Classical molecular Cytogenetics, FISH methods, Single molecule FISH (smFISH, MER-FISH, SABER-FISH, DNA paints Oligo FISH), Crispr based imaging, latest developments in high resolution chromatin imaging and its applications & Expansion Microscopy (7) Module:3: Functional Genomics: Bulk and Single cell approaches to study the genome and transcriptome: RNA Sequencing technologies, ATAC-Seq, Chip-Seq, Cut-Run, Cut-Tag, Transcriptomics, Spatial transcriptomics, Single cell omics technologies, Applications of systems & computational Biology, AI and ML in genomics (7) Module:4: Genome Biology & Disease: Inherited diseases, Metabolomics, Stem cells & genomics and Cancer, predictive diagnostics using omics and genome biology (7)

title: Satellite Data Analysis & Image Processing
objectives: Course open to 8th semester students and mandatory for MSc Geology students. Earth and Planetary Materials is a pre-requisite. The objective of the course is to provide a foundation in satellite-based remote sensing techniques for geoscientists. It has an equal emphasis on the physics of remote sensing, digital image processing of remote sensing data, and application of remote sensing. The course will cover the key image processing approaches and methods as well as hands-on exercises and analyses. The focus of the course is on the acquisition, processing, and analysis of remote sensing data and imagery in the geosciences. Principles of remote sensing learned in the course could also be applied to exploring planetary surface materials and surface processes. Students are encouraged to bring a topic of their interest and explore how they can utilize the concepts learned from the class to help them solve the problem of their interest. Expected outcomes: 1. Assess how image processing and image analysis may help solve a specific problem in geosciences or related disciplines. 2. Identify the appropriate imaging approaches or imagery pertinent to the problem. 3. Develop an approach and workflow that builds on the concept of the image-processing chain to move from raw imagery or geospatial data to a quantitative representation and digest information contained in the imagery. 4. Use their acquired proficiency in digital image processing to apply the appropriate tools out of the major image processing methods to solve geosciences, geospatial or societal problems. 5. Understand the limitations of the remote sensing technology.
course_content: A. Fundamentals of remote sensing: The Electromagnetic Spectrum, radiation units and laws, atmospheric scattering, BRDF, Albedo, path radiance, principles of spectroscopy and spectral signatures. B. Sensors and sensor technology: (i) Biology and optics of image processing, cameras, scanners and acquisition devices, digitization, satellite and airborne image acquisition differences and applications. (ii) Geometric transformations, interpolation, geometric operations, and projections. C. Understanding digital image: image, imaging, computer vision, image histogram, global and local thresholding, gradient detection, linear filtering: neighborhoods, kernels, convolutions, and their application on satellite images, frequency-domain operations, image fusion. Tutorial: Spatial and frequency domain filtering. D. Processing of colour and multi-spectral images: Colour representation and transforms, multispectral transforms (principal component analysis and minimum noise fraction transformation) and classification (unsupervised and supervised techniques). Tutorial: Pre-processing VNIR/SWIR data, image rectification (geometric and radiometric corrections), multispectral image analysis, image classification. E. Thermal remote sensing: Principles, atmospheric corrections, temperature Estimation, sub-pixel temperatures and atmospheric corrections, emissivity and applications of thermal remote sensing. Tutorial: Thermal image processing of ASTER images. Determine emissivity.
evaluation: / Assessment Evaluation / Assessment End-Sem Examination-35-40% Mid-Sem Examination-35-40% Others-30-20%

title: Advanced Molecular Spectroscopy
prerequisites: Fundamentals of Molecular Spectroscopy or similar course
objectives: This course mainly deals with a detailed understanding of lasers and the applications of various advanced spectroscopic techniques in chemistry, physics, material science, and biology. This course will be useful for students who would like to use a variety of advanced spectroscopic techniques for their research. The first part of the course will be an introduction to the fundamental concepts of light-matter interaction, optics, lasers and laser systems, and other relevant aspects of instrumentation necessary for spectroscopy. In the second part of the course, we will discuss various modern spectroscopic techniques and their applications. Discussion of each technique will be followed by examples from classic and contemporary literature.
course_content: Section #1- Light and its interaction with matter Topics: Introduction to electromagnetic radiation, Polarization of light, Linearly, circularly and elliptically polarized light, Absorption and emission of radiation, Einstein coefficients of A and B, Mechanism of light absorption and emission (Time-dependent perturbation theory), Selection rules, Line width and Line broadening. (6L) Section #2- Brief description of optics Various types of polarizers, Snell’s law, Brewster angle, Birefringence property of various crystals, concept of ordinary and extraordinary rays, classification of crystals, concept of various types of wave plate and their applications. (3L) Section #3: Fundamentals of lasers and laser systems Topics: (i) Introduction to Lasers, Concept of spontaneous, stimulated emission and population inversion. Mechanism of population inversion, two-level, three level and four level laser systems. Frequency and spatial properties of laser radiation. Components of laser resonator. Loop gain in laser resonator. Gain in continuous-wave and pulsed lasers, Q- switching and the generation of nanosecond pulses, Mode locking and the generation of picosecond and femtosecond pulses. (6L) (ii) Fixed-wavelength gas lasers: He-Ne, rare-gas ion, CO2 and excimer lasers, Fixed-wavelength solid-state lasers: Nd:YAG laser, Semiconductor diode lasers, Tunable dye laser systems, Tunable Ti:sapphire laser systems. (3L) (iii) Non-linear crystal and frequency-mixing processes, Optical parametric oscillation. (2L) Section #4: Advanced Laser spectroscopic techniques and applications Topics: (i) Cavity ring-down absorption spectroscopy, Laser induced fluorescence, Steady-state and time-resolved electronic spectroscopy, Transient absorption spectroscopy, Supersonic jet spectroscopy, Laser studies of photo- ionization (6L) (ii) Resonance Raman spectroscopy, Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS), (iii) Ultrafast spectroscopy, Laser Femtochemistry, Attosecond spectroscopy, Selected applications of lasers (7 L) (iii) Ultra-violet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS), Binding energy and chemical shift, Structure determination using XPS. (5 L)
evaluation: / Assessment a. Mid-sem examination- 35% b. End-sem examination- 35% c. Quizzes- 30%

Simon (Viva Books Private Limited, New Delhi, 1997) 4. Laser fundamentals: W. T. Silfvast (Cambridge University press, Published in South Asia by Foundation Books, New Delhi, 1998) 5. title: Discrete Structures (E )
objectives: Discrete Mathematics is the study of organizing finite sets. We may arrange them into graphs or partially ordered sets, for example. Enumeration is an important consequence of organization. Hence we study combinatorics and graph theory.
course_content: Combinatorics: Permutations/Combinations, Multisets, Poset Theory, generating functions, binomial coefficients, Mobius inversion, recurrence relations, Catalan, Stirling & Bell numbers, generating functions. Graph theory: Paths, cycles, bipartite graphs, trees.
evaluation: / Assessment 40% Midsem 40% Endsem 20% Quiz/Homework

title: Analytical Chemistry (E )
objectives: This course focuses on the fundamentals of analytical chemistry, covering essential concepts, methodologies, spectroscopy, separation and instrumentation techniques for analyzing chemical and biological molecules. This course will also cover advanced characterization tools and their applications in research and modern technology. Students will develop problem-solving skills and gain experience through class room demonstrations and exposure to advanced instrumentation facilities available at the institute. This course will strengthen analytical skill sets within the undergraduate curriculum. The course will be beneficial for undergraduate students interested in pursuing experimental research in chemistry, biology, physics and interdisciplinary fields.
course_content: Total contact hours: 30 h Topic-1: Introduction to Analytical Chemistry: (5 h) Quantitative and qualitative analysis, density and gravity of solutions* Chemical stoichiometry, volumetric calculations- normality and molarity* Titrimetric analysis: Theory and practice of titrations, including acid-base, redox, and complexometric methods. Statistical analysis: Handling and evaluating experimental data, including error analysis and method validation. Topic-2: Separation techniques for chemical compounds: (8 h) Solvent and solid-phase extraction* Extraction in laboratory and industrial scale. Chromatography-principles and theory: Column, Gas and liquid chromatography techniques, various types of Chromatography instruments (HPLC, chiral-HPLC, GPC) for both purification and estimation of organic compounds, proteins and polymers, etc. Size and shape-dependent, molecular weight- dependent, and charge-dependent isolation of proteins and polymers, natural products, etc. Extraction of medicinal products by imprinting technology, etc. Topic 3: Electrochemistry: (2 h) Principles of potentiometry, voltammetry, and electrochemical sensors. Topic 4: Spectro-analytical Chemistry (10 Lectures): Applications of spectroscopy techniques for the characterization and analysis of compounds by IR, Absorption, Fluorescence, NMR spectroscopy techniques and mass spectrometry techniques and their applications. (Please Note: only minimum amount of theory will be discussed in order to understand the instrumentation part. Students are encouraged to take the spectroscopy course for in-depth understanding on the principles of the techniques). Topic-5: Advanced Material and Biomolecular analysis techniques: (5 h) Thermal phase transitions in solids and amorphous materials, thermal stability and decomposition, determination of thermodynamic parameters. Zeta potential, Imaging of micron and nano-sized objects by electron microscopy (SEM and TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM), etc. Electrophoresis techniques, Isothermal calorimetric, Surface Plasmon Resonance, circular dichroism, etc. Tutorials: Data interpretation, class room demonstrations will be conducted by TAs. Exposure to advanced instrumentation facilities: These sessions will be conducted with the help of TAs/technical assistant. Hours spent on these training sessions will not be counted as part of the main lectures for attendance purpose.
evaluation: / Assessment 35% Mid-Sem* 35% End-Sem* 30% Two quizzes

Christian, Analytical Chemistry, Sixth Edition, Wiley (Indian Edition is available). (Most of the topics will be covered from this book) 2. Analytical Chemistry 2.1 by David Harvey. Can be download from: https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshel ves/Analytical_Chemistry/Analytical_ Chemistry_2.1_(Harvey) 3. Douglas A. Skoog, Donald M. West, F. James Holler, Stanley R. title: Condensed Matter Physics I
objectives: To learn the basics of elementary Solid-State Physics to see how the collective physical properties of a whole group of atoms and molecules as a *solid* become *not only more than but very different from the sum of its parts* - as described in a seminal article titled as *More Is Different* by the Nobel Laureate P. W. Anderson and published in Science, Vol. 177, No. 4047. (Aug. 4, 1972), pp. 393-396. We will learn the critical details of both theory and experiments of the topics mentioned below. These will be done to address how we model and estimate various physical properties of solids and how do we test the boundaries of these models using a variety of experiments.
course_content: 1) Crystal Structure & Reciprocal Lattice & Amorphous/Non- crystalline Solids. 2) Crystal Binding and Elastic Constants. 3) Vibrations of Crystal lattice, Photons and Thermal Properties 4) Density of States 5) Free Electron Theory 6) Formation of Energy Bands 7) Insulators, Semiconductors and Metals 8) Superconductors 9) Different types of Magnetism 10) Different types of Quasi-Particles in Solid State Physics 11) Dielectric Response 12) Magneto Resistance, Quantum Hall Effects, Spintronics etc. 13) Brief Descriptions of Exotic Phenomena at Nanoscale, MesoScale Structures, Soft-Condensed Matter Physics (If Time Permits!).

title: Algorithms
objectives: To provide a solid foundation in algorithm design and analysis. Student Learning Outcomes: (1) Ability to understand and design algorithms using the greedy strategy, divide-and- conquer approach, and dynamic programming. (2) Ability to analyze the asymptotic runtime complexity of algorithms, including formulating recurrence relations. (3) Basic knowledge of computational complexity and randomized algorithms.
course_content: Asymptotic order of growth: big O notation and its relatives. Divide and Conquer and Recurrences: The master theorem, application to the complexity of recursive algorithms, stable matching Data Structures: Stacks, queues, priority queue, heaps, binary search trees. Basic Algorithms: breadth first search, depth first search, DAGs (directed acyclic graphs) and topological ordering, strongly connected components. Greedy Algorithms: interval scheduling, Dijkstra*s algorithm for finding shortest paths in a graph, minimum spanning trees, The travelling salesman problem, Huffman codes for data compression. Dynamic Programming: Min Edit distance, weighted interval scheduling, subset sums and knapsacks. Weighted independent set in tree, longest monotonically increasing subsequence. Randomized Algorithms: Global min-cut, Quick Sort, randomized approximation algorithm for max 3-SAT. Introduction to NP-completeness* approximation algorithms.
evaluation: / Assessment Mid Sem: 35% End-Sem 45% Quiz: 20%
readings: (1) H. Cormen, C. E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, Clifford Stein, Introduction to Algorithms, 2/e, MIT Press (2) J. Klienberg and Eva Tardos: Algorithm Design. 1st Edition. Addison Wesley, 2005. ISBN 0-321-2 (3) S. Dasgupta,C. H.Papadimitriou,and U. V. Vaziran, Algorithms, McGraw-Hill Higher Education, 2006.

title: Principles of Organic Chemistry II (E)
prerequisites: CH1113- Principles of Organic Chemistry I, Basic Knowledge in Organic Chemistry and its Concepts
objectives: This course brings an exposure to the various concepts for the organic transformation and reaction mechanism. To gain exposure on the reagents and reactive intermediates commonly found in the basic organic reactions, which will be useful for making of the C-C/X bond formation reactions. The expected outcome of this course would be strengthening of foundations of organic synthesis* students would be in a position to rationalize the fundamentals of organic reactions and methodologies. Also, would appreciate the organic reactions that are vital to the life as biochemical processes.
course_content: Section 1: Brief History of Chemistry and Basic concepts in Stereochemistry: (8 h) History of Chemistry (including Stereochemistry), Carbonyl compounds and addition to Carbonyl groups, Stereochemical descriptors (briefly), enantiomeric excess, diastereomeric excess, chirality in nature* basics of carbonyl chemistry* addition to carbonyls* Stereochemical considerations and models for addition to carbonyl compounds. Section 2: Enols, Enolates and Other Related Reactions (9 h): Keto-enol tautomerism* Enolate chemistry: Keto-enol tautomerism* Enolate chemistry: Active methylene compounds* Enol and enolate equivalents* Kinetic versus thermodynamic enolate formation* Aldol reactions, Cannizzaro reaction* Mukaiyama Aldol Reaction* Enamine Reaction* Other active methylene groups* Acylation of enols and enolates* Reformatsky reaction* Claisen condensation* Benzoin condensation* Robinson annulation* Stobbe condensation* Dieckmann condensation* Knoevenagel condensation* Henry reaction Section 3: Molecular Rearrangements and Named reactions (8 h): Types of rearrangements* Rearrangement to electron- deficient Carbon, nitrogen and Oxygen, Migratory aptitude, Benzidine, Pinacol, Benzilic acid, Favorskii, Wolff, Hofmann, Curtius, Schmidt, Lossen, Beckman, Baeyer- Villiger, and Wagner-Meerwein, Dienone phenol, and other related rearrangements* Section 4: Free radical reactions (3 h): Substitutions and additions, SET mechanisms, Rearrangements involving free radicals. (*Hours for each section may vary)

title: Quantum Information and Quantum Computing
objectives: This course introduces students to the fascinating world of quantum information and quantum computing. It is designed to provide both the breadth and depth necessary to help students grasp the foundational concepts, engage with current literature, and develop the skills needed to initiate research projects in this rapidly evolving field.
course_content: Information, Classical Physics, and Quantum Physics: Landauer’s principle, quantifying information, Shannon entropy and properties, QM postulates, qubits and ensembles, state vectors and density operators, Bloch sphere, measurements, unbiased bases, entangled states, von Neumann entropy and properties, joint, conditional, and relative entropies, mutual information, partial trace and reduced density operator, no-go theorems, quantum key distribution, quantum gates, distance measures for states/gates, quantum control, quantum eraser & GHJW theorem, channel capacity, data compression, Schumacher encoding, Holevo bound, Fano inequality. Quantum measurements: Projective measurements, generalized measurements, POVM, Neumark theorem, state discrimination, realizing, general observables, interferometric measurement, joint measurements, pre/post-selection, quantum pigeonhole effect, invasive and noninvasive measurement, interaction- free measurement, weak measurements, state & process tomography, shadow tomography, quantum sensing. Quantum Correlations: Local macrorealism vs nonlocal objectivism, Leggett-Garg, inequalities, quantum contextuality, entanglement, Bell- CHSH inequality, Alain Aspect experiment, Schmidt decomposition, entanglement witness, entanglement entropy, Bell basis measurement of two qubits, entanglement distillation, superdense coding, quantum teleportation, E91 QKD, mixed-state entanglement, entanglement hierarchy, quantum thermodynamics, ergotropy, quantum battery, quantum thermal diode & transistor. Quantum computing algorithms: Turing Machine and complexity classes, DJ algorithm, BV algorithm, quantum Fourier transform, phase estimation, order-finding quantum algorithm, Shor’s factoring algorithm, period-finding algorithm, Kitaev’s algorithm, Grover’s algorithm, quantum counting, quantum simulations, adiabatic algorithms, variational algorithms, feedback algorithms, quantum games, reservoir computing. Noise, decoherence, and error correction: Superoperators, operator-sum representation, complete positivity, depolarization, phase damping, amplitude damping, Markov process, master equations, decoherence- free subspaces, dynamical decoupling, noise spectroscopy, quantum error correction, bit flip code, phase flip code, Shor code, five-qubit code, seven-qubit code, stabilizer codes, Knill-Laflamme theorem.
evaluation: / Assessment Quizes (best 3 of 4) : 30% Midsem exam : 35% Endsem exam : 35%.
readings: M. A. Nielsen and I. L. Chuang, Quantum Computation and Quantum Information, Cambridge (2000) J.Preskill, Quantum Information and Quantum Computation, Lecture notes (Caltech) : Available online Quantum Information, Stephen M. Barnett, Oxford University Press, 2009. An introduction to Quantum Computing, P. Kaye, R. Laflamme and M. Mosca, Oxford Unviserity Press. Quantum Information Theory* 1st Edition by Mark M. Wilde, Cambridge University Press, 2013 Classical and Quantum Information Theory: An Introduction for the Telecom Scientist* Emmanuel Desurvire, Cambridge University Press, 2009.

title: Indian geology and resources
objectives: Geologically speaking, India has everything : All seven types of Sedimentary Basins including Rift, Strike Slip, Passive Margin, Island Arch, Fold and Thrust Belts, Foreland, Intra-cratonic etc., representing full tectonic cycle. It has Mountain ranges : Young and old, fold mountain ranges ,volcanic mountain ranges. It has cratons: massifs and Pleatues. It has rivers: mighty, not so mighty, old and young. It has glaciers, deltas with associated landforms It has a vast coastline with big, commercial petroliferous basins Economically, it has huge deposits of coal, oil, gas, gold, iron, diamond, rare earths etc . etc. In fact, the Geology of India is diverse . Different regions of India contain rocks belonging to different geological periods dating as far back as Eoarchean (including oldest dated rock on Earth) to Recent including the Alluvium that has yet to undergo diagenesis . Thus, the understating and knowledge of the Geology of India and its resources are very helpful and to some extent mandatory to the students of Indian Geology. The main objective of the course is to provide a concise account of the diverse Geology of India, including chief geological and tectonic provinces ,evolution of the continent through geological times, origin ,distribution ,facies , stratigraphy and economic potential of each period and epoch aided by latest concepts in paleogeographic evolution, maps showing distribution of rocks , key field photographs and geological models for better understanding of the fascinating Geology of India. The economic geology part will emphasise on Mineral exploration scenario of India including Rare Earths – prospects, promises and policy reforms besides challenges in mineral intensive energy transition, Rare Earths and global oil scenario.
course_content: - Sedimentary Basins, classification, definition, types ,tectonic setting ,origin ,processes and sedimentary fills. - Summary of the Geology of Jaisalmer, Bikaner- Nagaur,Barmer, Cambay Basins , Basins of Western continental margin of India ( Kachchh, Mumbai offshore and Kerela-Konkan Basin ), East coast basins ( Cauvery, Krishna-Godavari, Mahanadi, Bengal ), Assam-Arakan and Tripura Fold belt in Northeast, , Ganga , Purnea , Vindhyan and Deep water Andaman Basin - Geological Divisions of India - Geology and Evolution of Himalayas - Archean and Dharwar groups - Cuddapah and Vindhyan groups - Cambrian,Ordovician ,Silurian ,Devonian , Early Middle Carboniferous of India - The Gondwanas - Upper Carboniferous and Permian of Extra-Peninsula - Triassic and Jurassic of India - The Cretaceous and Deccan Traps - The Cenozoic of India - The Shiwaliks - Economic Geology including Natural resources ( minerals) ,prospects, distribution, policy, including Iron, Base Metals , Gold, PGE deposits, Nickel , coal and detailed account of Rare Earths , hydrocarbon resources of India
evaluation: / Assessment Mid Semester Exams 40% End Semester Exams 60%
readings: Basic Textbooks including Geology of India by D N Wadia and M S Krishnan

objectives: This course continues from where Quantum Mechanics 1 left off. This will introduce advanced methods of quantum physics, and how it can be applied to problems of interest in physics. By the end of the course, student will have good working knowledge of quantum physics. Course is aimed at students who have already taken Quantum Mechanics I.
course_content: 1) Harmonic oscillator using operator techniques 2) Angular momentum, addition of angular momentum and spin 3) Hydrogen atom problem 4) Identical particles, spin-statistics connection 5) Time-independent perturbation theory and its application to atom in external fields 6) Time-dependent perturbation theory 7) Variational principle and application to solving for spectra 8) WKB approximation 9) Dirac equation (if time permits) 10) Introduction to quantum algorithms (if time permits)

title: Organometallic Chemistry
objectives: The main objective of this course is to help the students to learn the principles of organometallic chemistry with emphasis to the understanding of their structure, properties and applications. Organometallic chemistry has served as a bridge between traditional inorganic and organic chemistry and contributed to the development of several important discoveries in synthetic organic chemistry. At the end of this course students will have a thorough understanding of the classification and mechanistic aspects of several organometallic reactions and will be able be identify the role of organometallic complexes in organic synthesis and industrial applications.
course_content: Section #1- Concepts of Structure and Bonding: (4 hours) History, Definition, 18 electron rule, Classes of ligands, Bonding and structural considerations in organometallics and isolobal analogies. Section #2: Fundamental Reaction Process (8 hours) Oxidative addition and reductive elimination* Insertion and elimination* Ligand substitution processes, Transmetallation, Nucleophilic and electrophilic addition and abstraction. Section # 3: Preparative and characterization methods (5 hours): General methods for the preparation of organometallic compounds such as metal-hydrides, -carbonyls, -carbenes, -alkenes, -alkynes, -allyl and -arene complexes. Use of various spectroscopic and analytical techniques for the elucidation of structure, properties and reactivates of organometallic compounds. Fluxionally and stereochemically non-rigid molecules. Section # 4: Synthetic Applications: (9 hours) (i) Coupling reactions: Heck, Suzuki, Stille, Negishi, Kumada, Sonogashira, Buchwald, enolate, Hartwig, Hiyama, Tsuji-Trost allylation reactions. (ii) Cyclization reactions: cycloaddition (including click chemistry), Carbocyclization, electrophilic cyclization, Wacker cyclization. (iii) Addition reactions: Hydrometallation, Hydroalkoxylation, Hydroamination. (iv) Carbonylation: Pauson-Khand reaction, (v) Olefin oxidation, (vi) Carbenes, (vii) Activation reactions: C H bond activation, activation of CO and CO2, Section # 5: Industrial Applications:(2 hours) Hydrogenation, hydroformylation, isomerization, metathesis and polymerization reactions.

Elias, Basic Organometallic Chemistry, University Press, 2010.

title: Genetics(E )
objectives: The goal of this course is to build upon basic Genetics, which the students learnt in their high school and the first few semesters at IISER-Pune. This course will help revise basic concepts and then introduce advanced concepts. A strong emphasis will be laid on modern tools and techniques as well as the utility of model organisms.
course_content: 1) Mendelian Genetics 2) Non-Mendelian Genetics: Linkage, Incomplete Dominance, Maternal Inheritance, Extra-nuclear inheritance, Sex-linked inheritance, Sex determination, Dosage Compensation, Genomic imprinting 3) The Chromosomal basis of inheritance, Genetic Complementation and Mapping, Recombination 4) Bacterial and fungal genetics. Transposons, Vertical and Horizontal gene transfer. 5) Genetic screens as a basis for functional genomics. Mutagenesis screens, FLP-FRT & Cre- Lox Systems. 6) Gene isolation, manipulation and the techniques that revolutionized modern genetics. The utility of various model organisms such as bacteria, yeast, fungi, worms, flies, plants and animals will be discussed at different points during the course.

title: Mathematics Lab II
objectives: to learn computation
course_content: Lab II: Algebra Module 1: Solving a system of linear equations, elementary row-column operations, Gaussian elimination algorithm. Matrix Multiplication, addition, determinant, and inverse of a matrix. Module 2. Define vector spaces and linear transformations. Compute images and kernels and verify the rank-nullity theorem. Write the matrix of a linear transformation with respect to different bases and understand the change of basis. Module 3: Compute eigenvalues and eigen vectors of a matrix and linear transformation. Compute the Jordan canonical forms. Vectors, dot product, cross product, equations of lines and planes. Defining and plotting parametric curves, computing tangent vectors, lengths, curvature, and torsion of parametric curves. Module 4: Work with a polynomial ring over R, C, and F_q, find the root of a polynomial, factorize polynomials, and understand ideals. Work with the ring Z[i] and Z[ ?-5], factorize elements, find the prime ideals, etc. Determine automorphisms of quadratic fields. The ring Z/nZ and solving polynomial equations over Z/nZ. Module 5: Under subgroups, normal subgroups, conjugacy classes, centralizers, etc, for the following groups and compute sizes: symmetric groups, alternating groups, Q_8, dihedral groups, GL(2,q), SL(2, q), etc Module 6: Some basic algorithms: Euclidean algorithm, factorisation, Legendre symbols. Fermat’s little theorem, 2-square theorem, Wilson’s theorem, partition function.
evaluation: / Assessment Each module will be graded

title: Coding Theory
prerequisites: Although the mathematics courses of the first and second year of the BS-MS program are the only formal prerequisites, this course does require mathematical maturity, and must be viewed as an upper level mathematics course.
objectives: This course introduces the mathematical theory of error correcting codes. (Here coding does not mean computer programmming. Error correcting codes are the main systems used to ensure the high degree of reliability required in modern data transmission and storage systems. )
course_content: • Introduction: Need for coding theory. Some examples of codes. • Nonlinear Theory: Bounds on the size of codes, including asymptotic bounds. Shannon/Hilbert entropy function. • Linear Theory: Basic definitions, properties and problems. Basic theory of finite fields. Dual codes and MacWilliams identities. Automorphism groups. Cyclic codes. Perfect Codes. Overview of the problem of Decoding. • Geometric Theory: The projective geometric point of view of linear codes. Construction and properties of special codes and families of codes like MDS codes and Reed- Solomon codes. A flavour of Algebraic Geometry codes.
evaluation: / Assessment 40% for mid-sem and end sem, quizzes 20%
readings: 1. Introduction to Coding theory, J. H. van Lint, Springer Graduate Texts in Mathematics vol. 86, third edition. 2. Fundamentals of Error Correcting Codes, Cary Huffmann and Vera Pless, Cambridge University Press. 3. Introduction to Coding theory, Ron Roth, Cambridge University Press. 4. The Theory of Error-Correcting Codes, MacWilliams and Sloane (North-Holland Mathematical Library)
