title: Combinatorial Optimization
objectives: Linear Programming and Integer Linear Programming has emerged as powerful techniques in Combinatorial Optimization with applications in various fields. In this course, we will learn fundamental results about these techniques and their application to design approximation algorithms.
course_content: The first part of the course (for about 8 weeks) follows Suggested Reading-1 in its entirety. We might refer to Suggested Reading-2 for more rigorous treatment for some topic. For applications to approximation algorithms (for about 4 weeks), we will follow Chapters 12-15 in Suggested Reading-3. The detailed content is as follows: (2 weeks) Linear Optimization. It’s standard form and its Dual. Norm Minimization. Network Flow, Using Python and Gurubi to solve LPs. Geometric viewpoint of LPs and solutions. Basic Feasible Solutions and Extreme Points. (2 weeks)The Simplex Algorithm, A Sufficient Optimality Criterion. Anticycling. Obtaining a Basic Feasible Solution. Duality. The Strong Duality Theorem. Complementary Slackness. Duality for General Linear-Optimization Problems. Sensitivity Analysis. Right-Hand Side Changes. Local analysis. Global analysis. Column geometry for the Simplex Algorithm. (2 weeks) Large-Scale Linear Optimization. Decomposition. Reformulation via the representation theorem. Solution of the Main Problem (M) via the Simplex Algorithm. Lagrangian Relaxation. The Cutting-Stock Problem. (2 weeks) Integer-Linear Optimization. Some structured models. Unimodular basis matrices and total unimodularity. Consequences of total unimodularity. Modeling Techniques. Branch-and-Bound. Cutting Planes. Branch-and-Cut (4 weeks) Applications to approximation algorithms. Revisiting the LP-duality theorem. Min–max relations and LP-duality Two fundamental algorithm design techniques and the notion of integrality gap. Set Cover via Dual Fitting Dual-?tting-based analysis for the greedy set cover algorithm. Rounding Applied to Set Cover. A simple rounding algorithm, Randomized rounding, Half-integrality of vertex cover. Set Cover via the Primal–Dual Schema. Overview of the schema. Primal–dual schema applied to set cover.
evaluation: / Assessment End Sem % = 40 Mid Sem % = 30 Others % = 30 (3 quizzes with programming component)
readings: 1. *A First Course in Linear Optimization* by Jon Lee. Online accessible at: https://github.com/jon77lee/JLee_Line arOptimizationBook/blob/master/JLee.4.08.7z 2. “Combinatorial Optimization:Theory and Algorithms” by Bernhard Korte Jens Vygen (Fifth Edition) Online accessible at: https://www.mathematik.uni- muenchen.de/~kpanagio/KombOpt/book.pdf 3. Approximation Algorithms by Vijay Vazirani Online accessible at: https://ics.uci.edu/~vazirani/book.pdf

title: Geological Field Training
prerequisites: Basics of General Geology, and Sequence Stratigraphy
objectives: For geologists, field geological mapping is fundamental to understand the basics of Geology. For them, the field is best laboratory to test the concepts of scales of observation and related concepts. With its classic and easily approachable outcrops of Tethyan and Indo-pacific realms covering Jurassic to Recent sedimentary successions, Kachchh basin provides a unique opportunity to understand geological history and evolution of India since Jurassic. This field geological course in Kachchh is designed with the objective to train the students in various aspects of field geological mapping, tectonics, basin evolution * understanding fundamentals of sedimentology, clastic and carbonate facies, sedimentary structures, processes and products of various depositional environments including fluvial, delta, estuary, coastal, carbonate shelf and coral banks among others* fundamentals of paleobiology , micro and mega fossils biofacies, age and environmental interpretations *Peleogeographic reconstructions* fundamentals of sequence stratigraphy including mapping of sequences, sequence stratigraphic surfaces, concepts of scales, systems tracts at various hierarchical scales and different elements of petroleum systems including source and reservoir rocks etc. It also provides an opportunity to relate the outcropping successions with the homotaxial proved petroleum systems in the Kachchh offshore. This course will be useful for all Geology students in their professional and academic pursuits.
course_content: The course will begin with an introductory lecture about the basin Geology, stratigraphy, evolution etc, followed by 6 days field trip to classic sections
evaluation: / Assessment The evaluation will be done based on post field project work including field diary, field exercises ( 40%) and post field work assignments and viva (60%).
readings: Field Guide book provided by instructor.

title: Introduction to Indian Writing in English: Prose and Poetry
prerequisites: This is a reading heavy course, so an interest in studying literature is a must. Since the readings are exclusively in English, a high comfort level with English will be necessary.
objectives: To introduce students to a set of carefully selected, insightful, and important literary texts written by Indian authors in English (or translated into English) and through these: 1. To learn about literary concepts like genre, figurative language, and other formal aspects of literature 2. To learn to critically appreciate literary text for style and content 3. To re-evaluate familiar cultural, linguistic, and historical notions through these texts 4. To engage with relevant frameworks like post- colonialism, feminism etc.
course_content: The first few lectures will serve to introduce some salient aspects of IWE (Indian Writing in English), with an emphasis on geneses, early confluences and conflicts, and an overall background of the field. The rest of the lectures/discussions will be a mix of theory and literary texts. For the most part, the theoretical/historical background relevant to the primary text(s) will precede the discussion of the text in question. Students are expected to carefully read all the texts scheduled before they come to the class. Literary readings include 30 odd poems, 8-10 short stories, 1 play, and 1 novel. These will be provided as the semester progresses.
evaluation: / Assessment Mid-sem in-class assignment: 35% End-sem in-class assignment: 35% In-class participation: 30& [For PhD students, extra readings, classes, and assignments will be given]
readings: Will be provided by the instructor

title: Structural Methods and Analysis
objectives: • To learn theories of the major instrumental methods (spectroscopic and analytical methods) used routinely by organic chemists. • (IR, UV, Fluorescence, MS and NMR) used for structure elucidation in organic chemistry. • To develop critical thinking and problem-solving skills with discussions of spectra of known and unknown compounds. • To develop the necessary level of structural elucidation ability for each researcher for their day to day analysis of new molecules.
course_content: Section 1: Introduction of the course: Objectives and Outlook [1 h] Topics: Why would we study the course? Section 2: IR Spectroscopy [4 h] Topics: Instrumentation* Principles (Selection Rules and Hooke*s Law* Characteristic Group Vibrations) and Analysis of spectra. Section 3: Ultra Violet-Visible Spectroscopy [4 h] Topics: Interaction of electromagnetic radiation with molecules, Double Bond Equivalents, Instrumentation* Principles (Selection Rules and the Beer-Lambert Law* Chromophores), pH, solvent and substituent effects and Applications. Section 4: Fluorescence Spectroscopy [3 h] Topics: Basic principle* factors influencing fluorescence properties. Application of fluorescence: fluorescence sensing by energy transfer and pH* solvatochromic fluorophores* conformational sensitive fluorescent molecules* probes for diagnosis. Section 5: Mass Spectrometry [4 h] Topics: Methods of ionization, separation and registration of ions* The main rules of fragmentation of organic compounds* The approaches to the interpretation of mass spectra* High resolution mass spectrometry* The main pathways of fragmentation of the principal classes of organic compounds in conditions of electron ionization* Mass spectrometry of high molecular mass compounds and biomolecules (electrospray ionization, matrix assisted laser desorption/ionization). objectives: The objective of this course is to teach the fundamentals of major branches of Spectroscopy and its applications. title: Partial Differential Equations
objectives: A solution of an ODE is typically specified by finitely many constants. In contrast, a solution to a PDE is typically specified by finitely many functions. Both initial conditions of time and boundary conditions of space play a decisive role. PDE theory is thus much vaster than the theory of ODEs. This course focuses on four primary PDEs arising from nature: the transport equation, Laplace’s equation, heat equation, and wave equation, representing first order PDEs, second order elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic PDEs, respectively. Each of these leads to its own branch of mathematics that one could study for a lifetime. As well as being an engaging mathematical topic in its own right, PDE theory is essential for many topics in analysis, geometry, probability theory, mathematical physics, etc. Students of PDEs may go on to study topics like harmonic analysis, geometric analysis, operator theory, control theory, differential geometry, and the calculus of variations.
course_content: Introduction and Classification of PDEs (1 lectures) Transport Equation: Initial Value Problem, Non- homogeneous problem (6 lectures) Laplace’s Equation: Fundamental Solution, Mean Value Property, Maximum Principles, Properties of Harmonic functions, Green’s function, Energy Methods (6 lectures) Heat Equation: Fundamental Solution, Mean Value Property, Maximum Principles, Properties of solutions, Energy Methods, (6 lectures) Wave Equation: Solution by Spherical Means, Non- homogeneous problem, Energy Methods. (6 lectures) Solution via Separation of Variables (2 lectures)
evaluation: / Assessment 2 Quiz- 15+15 Mid-sem examination- 35 End-sem examination- 35
readings: 1. Partial Differential Equations: L. Evans (2010) Graduate Studies in Mathematics, AMS 2. An Introduction to Partial Differential Equations: Y. Pinchover and J. Rubinstein (2005) Cambridge

title: Mathematical and Statistical Optimization
prerequisites: Linear algebra, probability, statistics (at the level of IISER- Pune mandatory courses)
objectives: This course will be taught by Amit Apte and guest faculty Mihir Arjunwadkar. Anybody who intends to work with data and has a problem to solve and a question to answer using statistics and computation will benefit from the course. As data science / statistical science practitioners, we view mathematics as a concise, precise and powerful language capable of capturing/describing/modeling patterns. In this course, we focus more on concept and less on mathematical rigour, more on being able to apply/translate the conceptual framework to a concrete data-based problem and less on formal proofs. That said, some familiarity and comfort with the mathematical style of approaching a problem would be necessary. A solid grounding in the conceptual and operational aspects of probability theory and calculus would be assumed. Any data-based approach inevitably involves computing. As such, some proficiency in any programming language and familiarity with the process of translating a method or a problem into code is necessary. We will use python and R (and their notebook/markdown variants) for class illustrations and assignments.
course_content: 1. Motivating examples: PCA, least squares problems, regularization in optimization problems 2. Numerical linear algebra and matrix decompositions: stability, conditioning, and algorithms for SVD, QR, spectral, factorizations and their applications 3. Optimization as a recurring theme in statistical learning / inference and data science* fundamental notions of statistical inference, estimation, loss and risk, likelihood maximization, posterior maximization, bias-variance trade- off as the basis for nonparametric statistics, model and feature selection methods 4. Monte Carlo methods: A broad overview of methods requiring or based on randomness* specifically, Monte Carlo integration and Markov chain Monte Carlo.
evaluation: / Assessment This will be taught by Amit Apte and guest faculty Mihir Arjunwadkar. Mid-sem: 45% End-sem: 35% Quizzes: 20%

title: Quantum Chemistry
objectives: The objective of this course is to understand the rules governing the behavior of molecules and atoms – the theory of quantum mechanics – and thereby get a rigorous introduction to, what is perhaps, the most widely applied theoretical framework of chemistry. The course starts by discussing the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics with an emphasis on the physical implications of this elegant, yet non-intuitive theory. It then applies quantum mechanics to simple model systems and eventually to atoms and molecules. It explores one of the most pervasive concepts in chemistry: the chemical bond. The ideas discussed in this course will be useful to those who wish to pursue further study in the areas of theoretical and computational chemistry, spectroscopy, molecular biology and materials science.
course_content: 1. Introduction to Quantum Mechanics and Quantum Chemistry (1hr) 2. Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (3 hrs) 3. Understanding the role of symmetry in Chemistry using the particle in a box (2 hrs) 4. Harmonic Oscillator (5 hrs) A. Classical harmonic oscillator B. Quantum mechanical harmonic oscillator C. Ladder operators and commutation rules D. Harmonic oscillator as a model for molecular vibration E. Multi-dimensional systems 5. Rigid Rotor (3 hrs) A. Angular momentum B. Energy and wavefunctions of the rigid rotor, rotational constants C. Rigid rotor as a model for molecular rotation 6. Hydrogen Atom (3 hrs) A. Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom B. Energy and wavefunctions 7. Approximate Methods (5 hrs) A. Variational method B. Perturbation theory 8. Many Electron Systems (10 hrs) A. Electron spin B. Antisymmetry, Slater determinants C. The 2-electron system D. Born-Oppenheimer approximation E. Valence bond (VB) theory F. Molecular orbital (MO) theory 9. Qualitative introduction to the Hartree-Fock approximation and beyond for many electron systems (2 hrs) The following will be introduced A. Hartree-Fock: Self Consistent Field Method, a solution for the Schrödinger Equation. B. Approximations beyond Hartree-Fock and the concept of electron correlation
evaluation: / Assessment a. End-sem examination- 40 % b. Mid-sem examination- 30% c. Quizzes – 30%

title: Philosophy of Science
objectives: The course provides a trans-disciplinary (philosophical) toolkit and informs how to apply it, enabling the students to understand what makes science possible and how to include and exclude the various elements in its discourse.
course_content: The course will be delivered in 2 parts. Part I Week 1 to 9* Part II Week 10-12. Week 1 — Why Philosophy of Science for Scientists? Toolbox: Knowledge organizers (trees, tables, networks) to map assumptions – axioms – definitions – hypotheses – theorems – facts. Week 2 — Measurement & Mediation: Direct/indirect measurement* instruments as cognitive media. Toolbox: Functions* error* proportional reasoning. Week 3 — Early Models: Geometry, Universals/Particular: How geometry and ancient engineering stabilize universals. Toolbox: Definitions vs theorems* types vs tokens* analysis vs synthesis. Week 4 — Logic in Practice: Induction, Deduction, Abduction: How scientists actually move between data and theory. Toolbox: Inductive patterns* deductive proofs/refutations* abductive IBE* falsification/justification. Week 5 — Demarcation & the Nature of Scientific Explanation: Science vs non-science* what counts as explanation. Toolbox: Syntax/semantics* covering-law vs mechanistic vs unificatory models. Week 6 — Theory Change: Popper ? Lakatos ? Kuhn ? Feyerabend: Growth, revolutions, and “research programmes.” Toolbox: Trees (lineages), networks (programme components), auxiliary elements. Week 7 — Realism, Anti-Realism, and Entities: What (if anything) do models make real? Toolbox: Types/tokens* entities vs theories* robustness as a criterion. Week 8 — Reduction, Emergence, and Complex Systems: Multi-level explanation and limits of reduction. Toolbox: Functions & groups (symmetry/constraints), dynamical systems (qualitative). Week 9 — Science as Mediated Cognition & Sub-Culture: Laboratories, inscriptions, simulations, and social norms. Toolbox: Networks (instruments–inscriptions–claims), tokens/types in data pipelines. Week 10 — Designing for NOS: From Stochastic to Schematic: Pedagogy: moving learners from pattern- spotting to rule-guided modeling. Toolbox: Knowledge organizers in lesson design* abduction tasks* proportional/statistical reasoning. Week 11 — Assessing Modeling, Evidence, and Uncertainty: Severe testing, error probes, and argumentation. Toolbox: Statistical/probabilistic reasoning* falsification/justification in classroom form. Week 12 — Consolidation:

title: Undergraduate Research Experiments
prerequisites: Though there are no pre-requisites, students will not able to credit this course along with Semester projects in Biology, in the same semester i.e., Jan 2026.
objectives: This course is aimed at teaching students the process of experimentation in biology. A few different techniques will be used to demonstrate the process of carrying out experiments and collecting, analysing, and interpreting data. The process of asking questions and designing experiments will also be discussed in the context of the experimental techniques being taught. Students will also understand sources of errors and approaches for troubleshooting in experiments. At the end of the course, students can expect to learn the process of carrying out experiments and interpreting the results in the context of the question being asked. These experiments will provide an exposure to experimental approaches in three different subareas of biology.
course_content: The course consists of 2 modules, each of 6 weeks. In each module, students will get an opportunity to learn new techniques and apply these techniques to address questions in biology. The organization of the course will be as follows: In addition to the weekly two-hour lab, there will be office hours (pre-announced 2 hours per week) for Faculty/TA consultation/trouble shooting discussion. Students are expected to carry out the experiments independently in the UG lab area, during the week and will report/discuss progress/doubts to faculty and TA during the office hours and the weekly lab hours. The experiments may be carried out in groups depending on the number of registered students and the experimental requirements.
evaluation: will consist of record keeping, presentations and/or viva at the end of each module. Modules details: MODULE 1: PCR based screening of infectious and genetic diseases This laboratory module is designed for undergraduate students interested in molecular biology and diagnostics. Through this module, students will gain hands-on experience with PCR techniques for screening infectious and genetic diseases. The module aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the principles and applications of PCR in the context of disease diagnosis. Objectives: 1. Understand the theoretical foundations of PCR and its applications in disease diagnosis. 2. Gain practical skills in PCR assay design, primer selection, and optimization. 3. Learn to prepare samples, set up PCR reactions, and analyze PCR products. 4. Understand the importance of controls and quality assurance in PCR-based diagnostics. 5. Explore the ethical, social, and economic implications of PCR-based disease screening. MODULE 2: Introduction to Drosophila genetics This laboratory module is designed for undergraduate students interested in learning about Drosophila as a model organism, specifically the utility of gain-of-function & loss-of- function genetics. Students will gain hands-on experience with Drosophila handling and setting up crosses through this module. Objectives: 1. Understand the theoretical foundations of Drosophila genetics. 2. Gain practical skills in handling flies and setting up crosses. 3. Confirm gain-of-function and loss-of-function of a gene using molecular techniques. 4. Quiz (crosses), presentation and evaluation of data. Evaluation / Assessment Module 1-50%* Module 2-50% The instructor will evaluate each module separately at the end of it. There is no separate mid-Semester/End-Semester exam.

title: Measure Theory and Integration
prerequisites: Real Analysis I, Real Analysis II
objectives: Measure theory is a generalization of the theory of volumes, one which is reconciled with the subtleties of analysis. Sets which are negligible for the purposes of integration have “measure zero”, and it is central to determine which these are. For instance, sets of rational numbers, and the Cantor set have measure 0, but the set of irrational real numbers has infinite measure. The theory of integration is central to analysis, being a method to assimilate quantities. Delicate problems with integrals, such as those arising in Fourier series, require more sophistication than Riemann’s naive integral. The Lebesgue integral focuses attention on the domain of a function, and defines the integral in terms of measures of subsets of that domain. The Lebesgue theory is now standard, and gives a robust way to handle limits of integrals. In particular this course introduces the famous “L^2”-spaces comprised of squareintegrable functions. This is the basic example of a Hilbert space, spaces which are studied more thoroughly in Functional Analysis. Many concepts in probability theory may be expressed in terms of measure theory, so this course is also prerequisite for Stochastic Processes.
course_content: Lebesgue Integration: Algebra and sigma-algebra of sets, Lebesgue measure, real valued measurable functions of a real variable, existence of a non-measurable sets, nowhere dense sets with positive measure, Cantor sets, Integrability, Monotone and Dominated convergence theorems, Fatou*s lemma, Vitali’s convergence theorem, Egorov*s theorem and Lusin*s theorem, change of variables, Jensen*s Inequality, General Measure & Integration theory: Definition and examples of general measures, integration of measurable functions, absolute continuity of measures, Radon-Nikodym theorem, outer measure, the extension theorem, L^p spaces, Holder*s inequality, Minkowksi*s inequality, convolution, differentiation of measures, product measure and Fubini*s theorem.

title: Computational Physics
objectives: This advanced course aims to give the students competence in the methods and techniques of calculations using computers. The student will get explore to 7 different computational algorithms/calculations in 6 different modules. At the end of the course the student is expected to have a hands on experience in modeling, algorithm development, implementation and calculation of physical quantities of relevance in interacting many body problems in physics. Both quantum and classical computational tools will be introduced. The hands-on-lab component is 4 to 5 hours a week during contact hours, which will be used in problem solving. The students student will come to class after going through recorded lectures. The expectation is that in future the student will be able to learn on his/her own any computational method that will be relevant the future problem solving (in research or otherwise) and extract reliable trustworthy results. The focus is on the methodologies to implement algorithms and designing multiple levels of checks and cross checks, which the student will develop by implementing the algorithm in the 6 modules.

Module 2 : 2.5 Weeks : Lattice Monte Carlo simulations using Ising model to understand phase transitions: Metropolis algorithm, understanding kinetic barriers, finite size effects, role of thermal fluctuations* calculating thermodynamic averages, Principle of detailed balance, Binders cumulant. Module 3: 1.5 weeks: Solving differential equations, Linear, non-linear and coupled differential equations, Module 4: 1 week Partial differenential equation. Module 5 : 1 week: Solving differential equations Schrodinger eqn. in Quantum Mechanics with Numerov’s algorithm Module 6: 1 week : variational principle. Module 7: 2.5 Weeks : Classical Molecular Dynamics simulations using Lennard-Jones’ potential, : Classical Molecular Dynamics simulations using Lennard-Jones’ potential

title: Public Policy I
objectives: This pair of courses will introduce a field of ‘Public Policy’ that would be very relevant for practicing scientists. The courses are designed especially for the students of physical sciences. The courses introduce concepts, theories, and models from the field of public policy. These theoretical inputs will be illustrated with case studies and case examples from public policy practice. These illustrative cases are specially curated for Indian students of disciplines other than social sciences. There will be separate components for presentation and discussion on such illustrative cases. These two courses are designed to be stand-alone, and students can opt for one or two courses in any sequence. The first course in this pair will focus on the early phases of the policy process—i.e., designing and making public policy instruments. The course will begin with introduction of foundational concepts from the fields of political science as well as public policy. It will then dwell on various fundamental aspects and dimensions of public policy process such as: policy problems, policy instruments, policy institutions, and policy environment. In the later part of the course the discussion will focus on models of the process of making public policies. The course involves explanations and discussions on abstract level concepts and models, though this discussion will be supplemented with examples and studies specially curated for Indian students. These case studies are not management-type case studies but illustrative case studies. The course is designed specifically for students who do not have exposure to social sciences.

title: Geodynamics-1
objectives: Relevance: Earth is a dynamic planet. Its interior is evolving in response to the primordial, acquired during its formation, and internally generated heat (e.g., Radio-active decay). Consequently, the processes operating on its surface and interior across spatiotemporal scales (e.g., ocean floor formation, mountain-building, mantle convection, volcanoes, earthquakes) control the distribution of natural resources and related hazards, ultimately controlling habitability. Objectives: The course provides a basic understanding of some of the dynamic processes, and their time evolution, operating inside the Earth, with a particular focus on the role of gravity and heat. It will also provide hands on experience on numerical modelling using existing open-source, free and community driven software. The primary objectives of the course are: 1. To understand the role of gravity and heat to the processes and largescale structure within and at surface of the Earth. 2. To connect the theories and models to real-world data with a hands-on experience. Outcomes: At the end of this course, a student will learn about: 1. Fundamental forces operating in the Earth that shape its interior, and surface (from mountains to ocean floors). 2. Conceptualize Earth as a reference to think/inquire about the internal dynamics of other planets in our solar system and beyond. 3. Hands on experience on the state of the art open-source and free tools/software in the geodynamics community.
course_content: 1. A brief review of kinematic plate tectonic theory and observations leading to its formulation. 2. Where do the forces for kinematics come from and what is geodynamics? 3. Geodynamics and Earth’s Gravity: Gravity and geoid height anomalies, and their implications for isostasy. Stress and strain, elasticity, mechanical state of the lithosphere (elastic thickness) and flexure, comparison with earthquakes (location, fault-plane/moment-tensor solutions). 4. Geodynamics and heat: Fourier’s law, heat transfer and energy conservation equation, thermal structure of the lithosphere and mantle, and linkage with seismic tomography and surface heat flow measurements. Steady- state thermal models of continental and oceanic lithosphere using LitMod2D_2.0 (https://github.com/ajay6763/LitMod2D _2.0_package_dist_users.git). 5. Geodynamics and rheology: solid-state creep, viscoelasticity, force balance and momentum equation. Thermomechanical structure of the lithosphere and mantle, and comparison with global seismic tomography. 6. Geodynamics and fluid mechanics: Stokes flow, coupled thermal and mechanical, thermomechanical, evolution of the lithosphere and upper mantle, and related non- dimensional numbers. Thermomechanical evolution models of lithosphere and mantle using LaMEM (https://github.com/JuliaGeodynamics/LaMEM.jl.git).

title: Advanced Material Science
objectives: This course is designed to provide the students with fundamental knowledge and advanced research oriented insights into functional inorganic materials. The topic includes polyhedral to crystalline solids, polar functional materials, magnetism and magnetic materials along with their underlying principles and their characterization using experimental techniques. Additionally, the students will be taught the principles and applications of different thermal analysis techniques. Emphasis would be given on understanding of thermal stability, phase transitions and decomposition behavior of materials through interpretation of few experimental data. This course is relevant to any students pursuing research in physical and chemical materials science. In the end, the student will have substantial knowledge on structure–property correlations that determine their functional and technological applications of advanced Inorganic materials.

Ferroelectric and ferroelastic substances and their characterization using electrical measurements and scanning probe microscopic techniques. Utilization of ferroelectrics in memory devices, sensors and energy harvesting applications. title: Cognitive Bases of Science
objectives: Course scope & questions. This course integrates philosophy of science and cognitive science with the explicit goal of modelling how science (and mathematics/engineering) is learned and practiced. We will ask: What is it about human cognition that enables STEM, beyond language and culture? Do scientific abilities have biological bases, and if so, why don’t other animals “do science”? Is science primarily social? Are contexts of learning the same as contexts of discovery and invention? Which cognitive parameters support scientific practice? Given these insights, what would it take to cultivate scientific temper for all? Throughout, we keep India’s science-education mandate in view and develop a theoretical foundation for modelling educational practice as an empirical discipline within a broader framework of modelling learning/cognition. Overlap note. Students who have taken Science and the World, History of Science, or Philosophy of Science may find Part 1 partially () repetitive (or a useful refresher). Students who have taken Introduction to Learning Sciences may find Part 2 partially repetitive (or a useful refresher).
course_content: The course includes three parts. Part 1 (15%) & 2 (25%) will be covered to ensure that the prerequisites for Part 3 are satisfied. Part 3 will occupy about 60% of the engagement. Part 1 — The Phenomena (15%) Historical & Philosophical Introduction to the Nature of Science will be used to familiarize what needs to be explained by cognitive models. Early models of natural explanation* ancient mathematics* early engineering practices* direct vs. indirect measurement* geometry* universals and particulars* atomism in science and mathematics* logic* inductive, deductive, and abductive reasoning* demarcation (science vs. non-science)* theoretical vs. empirical knowledge* science, engineering, and technology* models of explanation (e.g., hypothetico-deductive)* the nature of experiments* growth and development of scientific knowledge* revolutions and paradigm change* problem solving. Part 2 — Models of Cognition & Learning (25%) Biological bases of cognition* learning in animals and humans* behaviorism* cognitivism and information processing* language learning* constructivism and constructionism* visuospatial cognition* classical AI* social/situated cognition* enactivism and affordances* embodied, embedded, extended (4E) cognition* predictive- processing accounts* emergence* forward models* information theory* modern AI (neural networks and LLMs). Part 3 — Applying Cognitive Modelling to STEM Practices (60%) Children as scientists* implicit vs. explicit learning* direct vs. mediated cognition* science as subculture* roles of models, metaphors, and analogies in practice and language* procedural vs. declarative knowledge* competence vs. performance* model-based reasoning* theories, models, experiments, and phenomena* mathematical modelling* microworlds* niche construction* simulations* documentation and communication* institutionalization of practice and learning* media* memetics* stochastic vs. schematic models* prediction* parsimony* assumptions and conventions.
evaluation: / Assessment Active Participation in the class room and online Class Room (30) MidSem Exam (30) EndSem Exam (40)

title: Topics in Number Theory
prerequisites: Real Analysis - I and II, Complex Analysis (can be taken simultaneously with this course too)
objectives: This course is a friendly introduction to number theory to students who are familiar with basic real analysis and a little bit of complex analysis. The underlying theme of the course is to understand the distribution of naturally defined arithmetic sequences, for example, the prime numbers, prime numbers in arithmetic progressions, the sum of divisors function, the Euler totient function etc. These quantities often exhibit beautiful arithmetic and asymptotic properties, which can be understood with the help of functions such as the zeta function and more generally, what are called the Dirichlet L-functions. One of the most important topics in this course would be the prime number theorem and its analogues for primes in arithmetic progressions. Once we start viewing the Riemann zeta function from a complex-analytic perspective, we encounter several questions about the location of the zeroes of this function, its zero-free regions, ``explicit* techniques to evaluate the sums of these zeros, and how these are related to the distribution of prime numbers. This course can be a good companion course to complex analysis for students with an interest in number theory.
course_content: Part 1: Elementary methods in number theory (12 lectures) 1. Introduction to congruence arithmetic and arithmetic functions : the Mobius function, prime counting function, sum of divisors function, Euler*s totient function, the prime Omega function. Order and average orders of arithmetic functions via summation techniques (Abel summation, Euler- MacLaurin summation). 2. Prime numbers: investigating the distribution of prime numbers by using some functions of Chebyshev and von Mangoldt, statement and manifestations of the prime number theorem, primes in arithmetic progressions. Part 2: The analytic theory of numbers (12-15 lectures) 1. Introduction to the Riemann zeta function, Dirichlet characters and Dirichlet L-functions. Properties of these functions and relationship with the distribution of prime numbers. 2. The Wiener-Ikehara theorem, the method of contour integration, Functional equations, a complete proof of the prime number theorem. Part 3: Advanced topics (9 - 12 lectures) 1. The zero-free regions for the zeta function, Counting zeros and explicit formulas, Selberg class.

title: Linear Algebra
objectives: The notion of a vector space generalizes our familiar experience of three dimensional space, with its planes and lines, to any dimension. However in higher dimensions our geometric intuition should be supported with the mathematical theory of coordinates. In this course the main objects of study are finite dimensional space over real numbers, its subspaces, and linear transformations. Our tools are the algebra of matrices and vectors. By introducing the notion of an “inner product”, we may also speak of angles and magnitude in any dimension. The importance of linear algebra to mathematics cannot be overstated. Along with calculus, linear algebra is one of the two pillars of mathematics, and is fundamental to any student of science.
course_content: Vector Space structure on R^n, Matrix operations and systems of linear equations, Gauss-Jordan Elimination, Matrix Inversion, Determinants, Abstract Vector Spaces with Examples, Subspaces, Linear Combinations, Basis and Dimension, Linear Transformations and Geometry, Rank-nullity Theorem, Coordinates and Change of Basis, dot product on R^n, Orthogonality and Gram- Schmidt Process, Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors, Diagonalizability.

title: Computational Thinking for Science
objectives: One end goal of this course is to have students thinking about processes within Science, and using computing to get those processes done by machines. This course will introduce the basic triad of Algorithms - Expression - Execution as the foundation for practice of Science. At the end of the course, students will be able to identify systems that can be described as computational processes, structure their operations as “algorithms,” express them using the Scheme language, and finally execute them on a machine. They will be able to identify layers of abstractions that form the basis of software packages, called “Applications Programming Interface (API),” and decide the level of abstraction best suited to their needs.
course_content: Idea of computation: Viewing processes as physical states and state transitions – Turing machines model, or states and state transformations – ? calculus model. The concept of a universal machine. Introduction to algorithmic thinking: Learn to imagine a process execution from a machine point of view. Expressing algorithms to machines: Minimal programming language constructs to express machine executable processes. Enhancing the language using convenience constructs. Executing programs on machines: The hardware as a simple but tedious general purpose, i.e. universal, machine. The OS as a high level abstract machine for executing processes. Building abstraction layers (i.e. APIs): Computing systems in practice Expression: Computation models – Logic languages, OO languages* Computing services – Graphics primitives, networking (and web abstractions in particular), domain computing – mathematics (arbitrary precision arithmetic, symbolic mathematics), AI (TensorFlow), Quantum Computing (QisKit). Execution: Hardware enhancements: CPUs - 8 bits --* 16 bits --* 32 bits --* 64 bits --* ... Maths coprocessors, GPUs, super computers. Networks. Software enhancements for execution: OS, containers, virtual machines.
evaluation: / Assessment End Sem: 50% Mid Sem: 30% Assignments or quizzes: 20%
readings: Main text: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs by Hal Abelson and Gerald Sussman, MIT Press. (Full Text Online) Computer Systems - A Programmer’s Perspective by Randal Bryant and David O’Hallaron, 3rd Ed. Pearson.

title: Introductory Seismology (E )
objectives: This is meant to be an introductory course in seismology, one of the major branches of geophysics. The course aims to provide an overview of the science of seismology, preparing students for more advanced courses at the graduate level. Useful for all physics and math students interested in seeing how concepts of classical physics and applied math are used in the science of earthquakes.
course_content: i. Wave propagation concept (string example)* continuum mechanics for elastic solids* equation of motion* acoustic and elastic wave equations in isotropic media. ii. P and S waves* plane wave solution in isotropic media* energy in a plane wave* waves in a layered medium* Snell’s law* reflection and transmission of plane waves. iii. Surface Waves – Rayleigh waves in a homogeneous halfspace* Love waves in a layer over a halfspace* dispersion, phase and group velocity. iv. Normal modes or free oscillations – modes of a liquid sphere, introduction to modes of an SNREI Earth.
evaluation: / Assessment a) Continuous assessment – 30% b) Mid-sem examination – 30% c) End-sem examination – 40%
readings: 1. Shearer, P. M. (2009). Introduction to Seismology. Cambridge University Press. 2. Stein, S., & Wysession, M. (2009). An introduction to Seismology, Earthquakes, and Earth Structure. John Wiley & Sons. 3. Lowrie, W. and Fichtner, A. (2020). Fundamentals of Geophysics (3rd ed.). Cambridge University Press.
